summary:
The Tanzimat ('reorganization') was a period of sweeping modernizing reforms in the [[event:94|Ottoman Empire]] lasting from 1839 to 1876, during which the imperial government attempted to transform its institutions on Western liberal lines to stem the empire's decline and reassert its standing among the European powers. The reforms represent one of the most ambitious state-directed modernization programs of the nineteenth century.

The Tanzimat began with the proclamation of the Gülhane Decree (Hatt-ı Şerif of Gülhane) in 1839 under Sultan Abdülmecid I, which promised Ottoman subjects equality before the law regardless of religion, security of life and property, and regular taxation in place of arbitrary impositions. These were radical departures from traditional Ottoman governance. The subsequent Islahat Edict of 1856, issued partly under pressure from Britain and France following the [[event:2547|Crimean War]], extended and reinforced equality promises to non-Muslim subjects.

The reform program was implemented by a series of progressive bureaucrats, most notably Mustafa Reşid Pasha, Ali Pasha, and Fuad Pasha. It encompassed the creation of secular law codes drawn from French models, reform of the military and taxation systems, establishment of modern schools and universities, construction of railways and telegraphs, and the introduction of a new system of provincial administration. The first Ottoman constitution and parliament were established in 1876, fulfilling one strand of the reform program, though Sultan Abdülhamid II suspended both in 1878.

The Tanzimat's achievements were real but incomplete and contradictory. Modern institutions coexisted uneasily with traditional structures. The extension of legal equality to Christians and Jews generated resentment among conservative Muslims while failing to satisfy the nationalist aspirations of Balkan Christian populations who wanted independence rather than equal citizenship. The reforms ultimately could not prevent the further contraction of Ottoman territory or resolve the 'Eastern Question' that made the empire's fate a perpetual concern of European great-power politics.
